decks.name: Risk glossary — Foundations & measures
decks.description: 27 foundations & measures terms, each thoroughly explained.

decks.name: Foundations & governance
decks.description: The vocabulary and scaffolding every risk function shares.

decks.name: Risk glossary — Market risk
decks.description: 22 market risk terms, each thoroughly explained.

decks.name: Market risk
decks.description: Loss from moving prices: measures, methods and sensitivities.

decks.name: Risk glossary — Credit risk
decks.description: 19 credit risk terms, each thoroughly explained.

decks.name: Credit risk
decks.description: Loss from borrowers and issuers failing to pay: parameters, models, products.

decks.name: Risk glossary — Counterparty & XVA
decks.description: 22 counterparty & xva terms, each thoroughly explained.

decks.name: Counterparty & XVA
decks.description: Default risk on derivatives, exposure profiles and valuation adjustments.
decks.primerTitle: Counterparty risk & XVA in one page

decks.name: Risk glossary — Liquidity & ALM
decks.description: 20 liquidity & alm terms, each thoroughly explained.

decks.name: Liquidity & funding
decks.description: The risk of running out of cash, and of markets that won't trade.

decks.name: Interest rate & ALM
decks.description: Curve risk, duration, and managing the banking book's balance sheet.
decks.primerTitle: Interest-rate risk & ALM in one page

decks.name: Risk glossary — Capital & regulation
decks.description: 39 capital & regulation terms, each thoroughly explained.

decks.name: Regulation & capital
decks.description: The Basel framework: how much capital and liquidity a bank must hold.

decks.name: Regulatory landscape — EU · Italy · Middle East
decks.description: Supervisory architecture and regulatory landscape across the EU, Italy and the GCC / Islamic-finance world. Quick multiple-choice cards (~1 min each) plus a few open exercises (~30 min). Non-firm-specific market context for risk and quant interviews.

decks.name: ECB / SSM & internal models
decks.description: How euro-area banks are supervised and how their internal models get reviewed on-site — the work of acting for the ECB to check a bank's risk management.
decks.primerTitle: ECB / SSM & internal models in one page

decks.name: Risk glossary — Operational, model & stress
decks.description: 25 operational, model & stress terms, each thoroughly explained.

decks.name: Operational, model & conduct
decks.description: Process, people, systems, models and behaviour — the non-financial risks.

decks.name: Stress testing & scenarios
decks.description: Probing the tail beyond what statistical models capture.
decks.primerTitle: Stress testing & scenarios in one page

decks.name: FRM — the discipline
decks.description: What financial risk management is at the firm level: scope, the corporate vs banking split, and why firms bother to hedge.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Financial risk management' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: FRM — the process
decks.description: The risk-management cycle: identify, measure, prioritise, mitigate (avoid/reduce/transfer/retain), monitor — plus appetite, limits and the mitigation toolkit.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Financial risk management' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: FRM — practice areas
decks.description: How the FRM discipline is organised in a firm: hedging programmes, ALM, treasury, model governance and the regulatory frame.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Financial risk management' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Quantitative methods
decks.description: The statistics and stochastic toolkit behind every risk model.

decks.name: Hypothesis testing: errors and power
decks.description: A visual-first treatment of the Neyman–Pearson framework: the two-distribution picture, Type I/II errors, statistical power, and the α-β tradeoff — anchored by an interactive slider that lets you feel the geometry directly.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Statistical hypothesis testing' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Derivatives & pricing
decks.description: Instruments, no-arbitrage and the machinery of valuation.

decks.name: Portfolio & investment risk
decks.description: Diversification, factor models and risk-adjusted performance.

decks.name: Foundations
decks.description: Base concepts: what a time series is, its components, stationarity, autocorrelation, white noise and the random walk. Distilled from Wikipedia as a test of the article→deck pipeline.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Time series', 'Stationary process', 'Autocorrelation', 'Random walk' and 'Decomposition of time series' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Decomposition
decks.description: Splitting a series into trend, seasonal, cyclic and irregular parts: additive vs multiplicative models, classical decomposition, STL, detrending and seasonal adjustment. Distilled from Wikipedia.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Time series', 'Decomposition of time series', 'Seasonal adjustment' and 'Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess (STL)' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Forecasting
decks.description: Predicting future values: benchmark methods (naive, seasonal naive, drift, moving average), exponential smoothing and Holt-Winters, train/test splits, accuracy metrics (MAE/RMSE/MAPE) and rolling-origin backtesting. Distilled from Wikipedia.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Time series', 'Forecasting', 'Exponential smoothing', 'Moving average' and 'Mean absolute percentage error' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Models (Box–Jenkins)
decks.description: The ARIMA family and the Box–Jenkins workflow: AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA, SARIMA, identification with ACF/PACF, unit-root tests, and order selection. Distilled from Wikipedia.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Autoregressive integrated moving average', 'Box–Jenkins method', 'Autoregressive model', 'Moving-average model', 'Augmented Dickey–Fuller test' and 'KPSS test' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Spectral analysis
decks.description: The frequency domain: Fourier/spectral analysis, the periodogram, the Wiener-Khinchin link between autocovariance and spectral density, aliasing and the Nyquist limit, and wavelets for non-stationary signals. Distilled from Wikipedia.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Time series', 'Spectral density estimation', 'Periodogram', 'Wiener-Khinchin theorem', 'Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem' and 'Wavelet' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Multivariate
decks.description: Several series at once: cross-correlation and lead-lag, vector autoregression (VAR) and impulse responses, Granger causality, cointegration and the error-correction representation, and state-space models with the Kalman filter's predict-update cycle. Distilled from Wikipedia.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Time series', 'Vector autoregression', 'Granger causality', 'Cross-correlation', 'Cointegration', 'Error correction model', 'State-space representation' and 'Kalman filter' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Advanced
decks.description: Volatility models (ARCH/GARCH), multivariate and cointegrated systems (VAR/VECM), state-space/Kalman, the frequency domain, and how forecasts are evaluated and backtested — with the link to market-risk practice. Distilled from Wikipedia.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Autoregressive conditional heteroskedasticity', 'Vector autoregression', 'Cointegration', 'Error correction model', 'State-space representation', 'Kalman filter', 'Spectral density estimation' and 'Forecasting' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Fundamentals
decks.description: The bell itself: the PDF, the two parameters, standard normal and z-scores, symmetry / zero skew / zero excess kurtosis, mean=median=mode, support, the 68–95–99.7 rule and the key z-quantiles.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Normal distribution' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Theorems
decks.description: Why the normal is central: the Central Limit Theorem and its conditions, closure under addition and linear combinations, stability, the maximum-entropy characterisation, the MGF / characteristic function, and links to the lognormal, chi-squared, t and F distributions.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Normal distribution' and 'Central limit theorem' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Inference
decks.description: Using the normal on data: the CDF and error function, the Q-function and tail probabilities, reading z-tables, estimating μ and σ² by MLE with Bessel's correction and unbiasedness, the sampling distribution of the mean and standard error, and confidence intervals.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Normal distribution', 'Error function' and 'Standard error' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Applications
decks.description: The normal in practice: measurement error and natural phenomena, regression / Gauss–Markov errors, parametric (variance–covariance) VaR with z·σ, QQ-plots and normality tests (Shapiro–Wilk, Jarque–Bera), and the central fat-tail limitation for financial returns.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Normal distribution', 'Normality test' and 'Kurtosis risk' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Lognormal
decks.description: When the log is normal: positivity, right skew, the role in asset prices and Black–Scholes, the mean formula, and the multiplicative CLT.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Log-normal distribution' and 'Geometric Brownian motion' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Poisson
decks.description: Counting rare events in a fixed window: the single rate λ, equidispersion, the assumptions, links to the binomial and exponential, and where overdispersion breaks it.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — 'Poisson distribution', 'Poisson limit theorem' and 'Exponential distribution' — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Student's t
decks.description: The fat-tailed cousin of the normal: degrees of freedom, convergence to the Gaussian, small-sample inference, fat-tailed return modelling, and the variance caveat.
decks.attribution: Adapted from Wikipedia — "Student's t-distribution" and "Student's t-test" — licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

decks.name: Hiragana (gojūon)
decks.description: The 46 basic hiragana. Each kana is tested both ways — recall the reading from the symbol and the symbol from the reading — tracked separately.

decks.name: Katakana (gojūon)
decks.description: The 46 basic katakana (used for loanwords). Each kana is tested both ways (symbol→reading and reading→symbol), tracked separately.

decks.name: Cyrillic alphabet (Russian)
decks.description: The 33 letters of the Russian Cyrillic alphabet, grouped by how they relate to the Latin alphabet. Each letter is tested both ways (symbol→sound and sound→symbol), tracked separately.

decks.name: Greek alphabet
decks.description: The 24 letters of the Greek alphabet — names and sounds, with the math/science uses you already half-know. Each letter is tested both ways, tracked separately.

decks.name: Hebrew alphabet (alef-bet)
decks.description: The 22 letters of the Hebrew abjad (consonants; vowels are added as points). Written right-to-left. Five letters take a special final form. Each letter is tested both ways, tracked separately.

decks.name: Arabic alphabet
decks.description: The 28 letters of the Arabic abjad, shown in isolated form (letters connect and change shape by position). Written right-to-left. Each letter is tested both ways, tracked separately.

decks.name: Korean Hangul (jamo)
decks.description: The 24 basic Hangul letters (jamo): 14 consonants and 10 vowels. They combine into square syllable blocks. Each jamo is tested both ways, tracked separately.

decks.name: NATO phonetic alphabet
decks.description: The NATO/ICAO radiotelephony spelling alphabet — the code words used to spell letters unambiguously over radio. Tested both ways (letter→code word and code word→letter), tracked separately.

decks.name: IPA — International Phonetic Alphabet
decks.description: Core IPA: one symbol per speech sound. Each card has a real pronunciation recording (🔊). Tested both ways — symbol→sound name and name→symbol — tracked separately.
decks.attribution: Pronunciation audio and phone names adapted from Wikimedia Commons / Wikipedia (CC BY-SA).

decks.name: World capitals
decks.description: Capital cities of major countries across every continent. Term = country, answer = its capital.

decks.name: Country flags
decks.description: National flags as emoji. Each card is tested both ways (flag→country and country→flag), tracked separately.

decks.name: World geography
decks.description: The big facts: continents and oceans, plus the largest, longest, highest and deepest features on Earth.

decks.name: Periodic table — element symbols
decks.description: Common chemical elements, tested both ways: symbol → name and name → symbol, tracked separately. Grouped by where they sit in the periodic table.

decks.name: SI units & physical constants
decks.description: The seven SI base units and what they measure, the most-used derived units, and the major physical constants with their values.
decks.primerTitle: SI units & constants at a glance

decks.name: Metric (SI) prefixes
decks.description: The SI decimal prefixes, tested both ways: symbol → name & power of ten, and back. Tracked separately in each direction.

decks.name: Cognitive biases
decks.description: Systematic errors in human judgement and decision-making — what each bias is, with a quick everyday example. Flip cards plus a few 'which bias is this?' multiple-choice cards.

decks.name: Logical fallacies
decks.description: Common errors of reasoning — formal and informal — with Latin names where apt, a definition and a quick example. Flip cards plus a few 'name the fallacy' multiple-choice cards.

decks.name: Music theory basics
decks.description: Foundations of Western music theory — intervals by semitone, major and minor triads, the circle of fifths, note durations and key signatures. Flip cards plus a few multiple-choice checks.

decks.name: Chess basics
decks.description: Chess fundamentals — relative piece values, opening ideas, key tactical and rules terms (fork, pin, en passant, castling), and basic checkmate patterns. Flip cards plus a few multiple-choice checks.

decks.name: Big-O complexity
decks.description: Asymptotic complexity classes and the typical time complexity of common operations and algorithms — binary search, quicksort, hash lookup, sorting, graph traversal. Term: an operation or algorithm; definition: its Big-O plus a short note. Quick recall cards for interviews and day-to-day reasoning.

decks.name: Data structures
decks.description: Core data structures — arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, hash maps, trees, heaps, graphs — with their defining properties and typical operation complexities. A mix of flip cards (properties) and multiple-choice cards (discriminating between close structures).

decks.name: Git commands
decks.description: Everyday Git tasks mapped to the command that does them — staging, committing, branching, undoing, inspecting history, and working with remotes. Most cards give a task and ask for the command; a few run in reverse, showing a command and asking what it does.

decks.name: HTTP status codes
decks.description: HTTP response status codes grouped by class — 2xx success, 3xx redirection, 4xx client errors, 5xx server errors. Term: the code; definition: its standard name and what it means. Covers the codes you actually meet building and debugging web APIs.

decks.name: Regex tokens
decks.description: Regular-expression syntax, token by token — character classes, anchors, quantifiers, groups, and lookarounds. Term: the token; definition: what it matches and how it behaves. Based on the common PCRE / Perl-style flavour used in most languages.

decks.name: Statistical inference
decks.description: Hypothesis testing from the ground up: null and alternative hypotheses, Type I/II errors, the correct meaning of a p-value, confidence intervals, statistical power, and when to use a t-test versus a z-test.

decks.name: Linear algebra
decks.description: The matrix machinery behind quant methods: vectors and matrices, dot product, matrix multiplication, determinant and inverse, rank, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, positive-definiteness, and the singular value decomposition.

decks.name: Linear regression
decks.description: OLS and its diagnostics: the Gauss-Markov assumptions and when OLS is BLUE, residuals, $R^2$ vs adjusted $R^2$, multicollinearity, heteroskedasticity, overfitting, and ridge/lasso regularization.

decks.name: Monte Carlo simulation
decks.description: Estimation by random sampling: what Monte Carlo is, its link to the law of large numbers, the $1/\sqrt{N}$ convergence rate, variance-reduction tricks (antithetic and control variates), pseudo- vs quasi-random numbers, and use in pricing and risk.

decks.name: Stochastic calculus
decks.description: Brownian motion, Itô processes and SDEs, Itô's lemma and its drift correction, quadratic variation, and geometric Brownian motion — the dynamics behind lognormal asset prices and Black–Scholes.

decks.name: Copulas & extreme-value theory
decks.description: Sklar's theorem and the copula idea, tail dependence, the Gaussian/Student-t/Clayton/Gumbel families, and extreme-value theory (block maxima → GEV, peaks-over-threshold → GPD) — the tools for modelling joint and far-tail risk.

decks.name: Option Greeks & Black–Scholes
decks.description: The Greeks (delta, gamma, vega, theta, rho) with their signs and intuition, the Black–Scholes assumptions, implied volatility and the smile, put–call parity, and moneyness.

decks.name: Fixed income
decks.description: Present value and yield, the price–yield relationship, Macaulay vs modified duration, convexity and DV01, the yield curve and bootstrapping the zero curve — the mathematics of bond pricing and rate risk.

decks.name: Morse code (letters & digits)
decks.description: International Morse code for the 26 letters A–Z and the 10 digits 0–9, built from dots (·) and dashes (−). Each card is tested both ways (letter→code and code→letter), tracked separately.

decks.name: Braille alphabet (A–Z)
decks.description: The 26 letters A–Z in Braille, each a pattern of raised dots in a six-position cell (1-2-3 down the left column, 4-5-6 down the right). Each card is tested both ways (letter→dots and dots→letter), tracked separately.

decks.name: Kanji starter (JLPT N5)
decks.description: About 90 of the most basic kanji, the level expected for JLPT N5. Each kanji is tested both ways — recall the meaning from the character and the character from the meaning — tracked separately. Readings are simplified to one main reading.
decks.primerTitle: Kanji at N5 — how to read this deck

decks.name: C++ — Syntax, types & variables
decks.description: The starting point of C++: program structure, fundamental types, variables, const, references, type deduction with auto, and basic I/O with streams. Quick recall and concept cards for absolute beginners moving into C++.
decks.primerTitle: C++ syntax, types & variables — quick recap

decks.name: C++ — Control flow & functions
decks.description: Decision and loop constructs, then functions: declaration vs definition, parameters by value vs by reference, default arguments, overloading and scope. Builds directly on the syntax & types deck.
decks.primerTitle: C++ control flow & functions — quick recap

decks.name: C++ — Pointers, references & raw memory
decks.description: How C++ addresses memory: pointers vs references, the stack vs the heap, new/delete, dangling pointers and leaks. The raw-memory groundwork that motivates RAII and smart pointers in the next deck.
decks.primerTitle: C++ pointers, references & raw memory — quick recap

decks.name: C++ — RAII, smart pointers & move
decks.description: The C++ resource model: RAII, the smart pointers unique_ptr / shared_ptr / weak_ptr, and a first look at move semantics. How modern C++ makes manual new/delete unnecessary and leak-safe.
decks.primerTitle: C++ RAII, smart pointers & move — quick recap

decks.name: C++ — Classes & object lifetime
decks.description: Defining classes: members, access control, constructors and destructors, the member-initialiser list, the rule of three/five, and operator overloading. The foundation of object-oriented C++.
decks.primerTitle: C++ classes & object lifetime — quick recap

decks.name: C++ — Inheritance & polymorphism
decks.description: Inheritance, virtual functions and runtime polymorphism, abstract classes and interfaces, the importance of virtual destructors, override/final, and slicing. The dynamic-dispatch half of C++ OOP.

decks.name: C++ — Templates & generic programming
decks.description: Generic code in C++: function templates, class templates, template argument deduction, specialisation, non-type parameters, and how templates are compiled. The mechanism the STL is built on.
decks.primerTitle: C++ templates & generic programming — quick recap

decks.name: C++ — STL containers, iterators & algorithms
decks.description: The Standard Template Library: the main containers (vector, map, set, unordered variants), iterators, the <algorithm> header, and lambdas as the callable glue. How to write idiomatic, container-driven C++.
decks.primerTitle: C++ STL — containers, iterators & algorithms recap

decks.name: C++ — Modern: C++11 & C++14
decks.description: What the C++11 and C++14 standards introduced and why each feature beats the pre-C++11 way: auto, range-for, lambdas, move semantics, nullptr, smart pointers, constexpr, threads, and the C++14 polish (generic lambdas, make_unique).

decks.name: C++ — Modern: C++17, C++20 & C++23
decks.description: What the later standards introduced and why they improve on older code: C++17 (structured bindings, optional/variant, if constexpr, filesystem), C++20 (concepts, ranges, coroutines, modules, the spaceship operator) and C++23 highlights.
decks.primerTitle: Modern C++ (C++17 / C++20 / C++23) — quick recap

decks.name: Physical constants (SI · cgs · natural)
decks.description: Fundamental constants with their values across unit systems — SI, Gaussian-cgs, and natural units — plus why some are set to 1 and why dimensionless constants don't change.

decks.name: Probability
decks.description: Counting equally-likely outcomes: adjacency, gaps, hypergeometric draws, distinct-bucket products, dice.

decks.name: Complement & 'at least one'
decks.description: The 'at least one' trick: P(at least one) = 1 − P(none). Dice and coins, de Méré's paradox, and the expected-count-vs-probability trap.

decks.name: Probability games
decks.description: Coin-and-parameter problems in the style of the unfair-coin question. Common thread: p^a*(1-p)^b is maximised at p = a/(a+b), and the special case p*(1-p) <= 1/

decks.name: Random walks & Markov chains
decks.description: Return/target probabilities for uniform random walks on small graphs.

decks.name: Counting & combinatorics
decks.description: Combinations, permutations with repeats, stars-and-bars, derangements, complementary counting, lattice paths.

decks.name: Pigeonhole principle
decks.description: Worst-case guarantees: the minimum draws forcing k items in one hole.

decks.name: Digit & number puzzles
decks.description: Digit-product/-sum puzzles, two-digit algebra, units-digit cycles, inclusion-exclusion, digital roots.

decks.name: Pseudocode tracing
decks.description: Trace pseudocode to its output; recognise classic algorithms.

decks.name: Pseudocode completion
decks.description: Pick the line that makes a function compute its spec — factorial, sums, powers, odd sums, running max, even count, Fibonacci.

decks.name: Logic puzzles & brain-teasers
decks.description: Word-logic puzzles (ages, coins, weighings, knights & knaves, Hanoi, clocks) plus classic brain-teasers where the fast guess is wrong.

decks.name: Conditional logic chains
decks.description: 'Customer A buys apples; everyone who buys apples buys pears; ...' Deduce what must be true via modus ponens, the contrapositive, and disjunctive syllogism. Wro

decks.name: Bayes & conditional probability
decks.description: Updating beliefs given evidence: base-rate traps, the two-children/at-least-one subtlety, Monty Hall, and which-source posteriors. The intuitive answer is usually the pla

decks.name: Expected value & waiting times
decks.description: Recursion on states and linearity of expectation: waiting times for patterns, coupon collector, gambler's ruin (hitting probability and duration), and optimal stopping.

decks.name: Waiting times & arrivals
decks.description: When you sample a schedule at a random instant: offset timetables (the ice-cream/subway puzzle), exponential memorylessness, the waiting-time and inspection paradoxes, co

decks.name: The birthday problem family
decks.description: The collision probability and everything around it: thresholds for 50% and 99%, matching a specific date versus any two people, expected number of matching pairs, smaller

decks.name: Intransitivity & paradoxes
decks.description: Surprising results that defeat intuition: Penney's game and non-transitive (Efron) dice, Simpson's paradox, the two-envelope fallacy, the birthday problem, and the gamble

decks.name: Combinatorial games
decks.description: Two-player perfect-information games solved by finding losing positions: subtraction games and their modular patterns, misere play, Nim (nim-sum), subtract-a-square, and

decks.name: Market-making & EV
decks.description: Trader-flavoured expected value: fair value, edge, buying away from fair value, the beauty-contest equilibrium, EV-versus-risk, and spotting arbitrage from odds.

decks.name: Quant reasoning (finance)
decks.description: Desk-style reasoning: volatility drag and geometric vs arithmetic means, Kelly sizing, EV vs median, the Rule of 72, no-arbitrage forward pricing, diversification, and Sh

decks.name: Rates, mixtures & motion
decks.description: Word problems with the classic traps: average speed as a harmonic mean, combined work rates, successive-replacement mixtures, relative speed, and clock angles. SI units t

decks.name: Spatial & geometric counting
decks.description: Enumeration in geometry: painted-cube face counts, rectangles and squares in grids, polygon diagonals, and the circle-division 'almost powers of two' trap.

decks.name: Cryptarithms / alphametics
decks.description: Letters stand for distinct digits with no leading zeros; each puzzle has a unique solution found by carry-reasoning. Verified by exhaustive search.

decks.name: Weighing & information
decks.description: Counting how much information each action yields: balance-scale weighings (known and unknown direction), binary encoding (poison bottles, 20 questions), and reachability

decks.name: Invariants & colouring
decks.description: Possibility puzzles cracked by a conserved quantity: checkerboard colouring, parity of flips and moves, permutation parity, and piece-count monovariants.

decks.name: Interview question bank
decks.description: 150 multiple-choice + 10 open-ended questions for quant/data/risk screenings and interviews — stats, econometrics, risk, regulation, modelling, Python/R/SAS. Flip for an explained answer and sources.

decks.name: Causal traps & statistical paradoxes
decks.description: Regression to the mean, Goodhart's Law, Berkson's paradox, and collider bias — the four structural reasons a signal can deceive you. Each card presents a realistic scenario; the back explains the full reasoning and names the fallacy.

decks.name: Python for data (pandas)
decks.description: Python for statistics & risk data work: types, pandas DataFrames, joins, groupby, missing data, NumPy, scikit-learn, statsmodels — concepts then a which-function recall quiz.

decks.name: R for data (base · dplyr · DBI)
decks.description: R for statistics & risk data work: vectors, data.frames, factors, dplyr verbs, models (lm/glm), the apply family, DBI — concepts then a which-function recall quiz.

decks.name: SAS for data (DATA step · PROC SQL)
decks.description: SAS for statistics & risk data work: the DATA step, LIBNAME, PROC IMPORT/MEANS/FREQ/REG/LOGISTIC, PROC SQL, MERGE BY — concepts then a which-function recall quiz.

decks.name: OLS regression
decks.description: 25 interview-screen MCQs — ols regression. Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Regression diagnostics
decks.description: 20 interview-screen MCQs — regression diagnostics. Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Statistics & inference
decks.description: 26 interview-screen MCQs — statistics & inference. Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Panel data & causal inference
decks.description: 15 interview-screen MCQs — panel data & causal inference. Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Time series — fundamentals
decks.description: 25 interview-screen MCQs — time series — fundamentals. Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Time series — advanced (cointegration, GARCH)
decks.description: 20 interview-screen MCQs — time series — advanced (cointegration, garch). Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Market risk — VaR, ES & Greeks
decks.description: 24 interview-screen MCQs — market risk — var, es & greeks. Each card explains the answer.
decks.primerTitle: Market risk — VaR, ES & the Greeks

decks.name: Credit & other risk
decks.description: 15 interview-screen MCQs — credit & other risk. Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Portfolio & asset pricing
decks.description: 20 interview-screen MCQs — portfolio & asset pricing. Each card explains the answer.

decks.name: Applied cases
decks.description: 10 interview-screen MCQs — applied cases. Each card explains the answer.
decks.primerTitle: Applied cases — pick the right tool

decks.name: Nominal vs real, deflator & Fisher
decks.description: Decomposing nominal into real + inflation: GDP growth, the GDP deflator vs CPI, real returns, and the Fisher equation — one piece of algebra behind all of them.
